claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) enables pluggable, discoverable, and composable agent infrastructure; servers expose tools, resources, and prompts that clients can dynamically discover and use without code coupling.
actionable: When building multi-agent systems, use MCP servers to expose tools and data sources; clients discover capabilities via the capabilities endpoint and compose them without tight coupling.
source_title: Why MCP really is a big deal | Model Context Protocol with Tim Berglund

claim: MCP servers can be composed hierarchically; a server itself can be an MCP client to another server, enabling modular data pipelines (e.g., agent -> Confluent MCP server -> Kafka topics).
actionable: Design MCP servers that integrate backend systems; let agents query Kafka topics, databases, or file systems through MCP abstraction layers.
source_title: Why MCP really is a big deal | Model Context Protocol with Tim Berglund

claim: MCP addresses a gap in LLM APIs: models can declare tools natively (via provider API parameters) but cannot directly invoke them; the client code decides whether to call tools after the model recommends them.
actionable: Use MCP to ensure clients retain control over tool invocation; models recommend actions, clients execute and report results back.
source_title: Why MCP really is a big deal | Model Context Protocol with Tim Berglund

claim: MCP uses JSON-RPC over HTTP with Server-Sent Events for async notifications, enabling rich client-server communication; connection can also use stdio for local processes.
actionable: When implementing MCP servers, support HTTP+SSE for remote connections and stdio for local; JSON-RPC is the transport layer.
source_title: Why MCP really is a big deal | Model Context Protocol with Tim Berglund
